title: Interactive Demo Pattern
text:
Purpose
This page is a starter pattern for a browser-side interactive segmentation demo that you can adapt after adding the optional Shinylive extension.
The goal is not to reproduce the full tutorial here, but to provide a small interactive page suitable for GitHub Pages.

title: Interactive Demo Pattern
text:
Suggested setup command
quarto add quarto-ext/shinylive

title: Interactive Demo Pattern
text:
Minimal example structure
Once the extension is installed, replace this placeholder section with your preferred Shinylive block from the extension’s current documentation and keep the app intentionally small.
A good first interactive exercise is:

slider for sample size,
slider for number of clusters,
select box for data type,
reactive scatterplot of PCA coordinates.

title: Interactive Demo Pattern
text:
Why this page is separate
Keeping the interactive demo separate from the main tutorial helps in two ways:

the tutorial remains easy to render and debug as a normal Quarto document;
the browser-side demo can stay intentionally small so it performs well under Pyodide/WebAssembly constraints.

The Shinylive extension is specifically meant for embedding Shiny for Python apps that run entirely in the browser. citeturn231216view0

title: Interactive Demo Pattern
text:
Practical note
Not every Python package works equally well in WebAssembly/browser runtimes, so smaller demos with mainstream scientific Python packages are the safest fit. The Shinylive extension documentation explicitly notes that not all Python packages can run in WebAssembly. citeturn231216view0

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
This tutorial is organized around three common segmentation settings:

Survey data with attitudinal and preference variables.
Transaction data with customer-level behavioral features such as recency, frequency, and spending.
CRM data with mixed numeric and categorical account fields.

The emphasis is on a workflow that is browser-friendly and relatively Pyodide-compatible:

numpy
pandas
matplotlib
scikit-learn

That means avoiding packages that are excellent on the desktop but less convenient in browser runtimes.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
This tutorial is organized around three common segmentation settings:

Survey data with attitudinal and preference variables.
Transaction data with customer-level behavioral features such as recency, frequency, and spending.
CRM data with mixed numeric and categorical account fields.

The emphasis is on a workflow that is browser-friendly and relatively Pyodide-compatible:

numpy
pandas
matplotlib
scikit-learn

That means avoiding packages that are excellent on the desktop but less convenient in browser runtimes.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
What this tutorial does
For each data type, the tutorial will:

generate a synthetic dataset with a known latent segment structure,
preprocess the data,
compare candidate numbers of clusters,
fit a segmentation model,
visualize the result,
produce segment-profiling tables.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
Pyodide and Quarto notes
This file is written as a normal Quarto .qmd using executable Python chunks. It can be rendered locally with Quarto and Jupyter. It is also designed to be Pyodide-friendly in the sense that it relies on packages that are commonly available in Pyodide-based browser execution environments.
A few practical notes:

Keep datasets modest in size for browser execution.
Prefer matplotlib over heavier plotting stacks.
Avoid file I/O dependencies.
Favor scikit-learn pipelines and transformers over niche clustering packages.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text: Setup

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
1. Survey segmentation

Framing
Survey segmentation is common when you have attitudinal or preference variables such as:

price sensitivity,
quality emphasis,
environmental concern,
novelty seeking,
channel preference.

In practice, survey segmentation often uses ordinal scales or one-hot encoded categories. To keep this tutorial Pyodide-friendly, we will build a synthetic survey with Likert-like numeric items and use K-means after standardization.

Interpretation
A typical interpretation here would be:

the segment with high price_importance and lower brand_loyalty is a value-oriented segment,
the segment with high quality_importance and brand_loyalty is a premium/loyal segment,
the segment with high innovation_interest and social_media_engagement is a trend-seeking segment.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data

2. Transaction segmentation

Framing
Transaction segmentation usually starts with customer-level feature engineering rather than raw order lines. Common features include:

recency,
purchase frequency,
monetary value,
average order value,
discount reliance,
category breadth.

This section generates synthetic RFM-style data plus two additional behavioral variables.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
3. CRM segmentation with mixed data

Framing
CRM and account-level segmentation often combines:

numeric variables such as tenure, spend, and support load,
categorical variables such as region, plan type, channel, and industry.

A mixed-type workflow can be handled cleanly with a ColumnTransformer that standardizes numeric columns and one-hot encodes categorical columns, followed by clustering.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
5. Recommended adaptation patterns

For real survey data
Replace the synthetic survey with your real table and:

map Likert items to numeric scores,
consider one-hot encoding for purely nominal variables,
compare KMeans with AgglomerativeClustering.



For real transaction data
Build a customer-feature table first:

recency,
purchase frequency,
spend,
average order value,
category breadth,
discount share,
return rate,
tenure.

Then:

log-transform highly skewed variables,
standardize,
test multiple values of k,
profile the segments in business language.



For real CRM data
Use a mixed-data preprocessing pipeline:

impute missing values,
scale numerics,
one-hot encode categoricals,
cluster transformed features,
summarize both means and top categories by segment.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data

6. Optional: hierarchical clustering for survey data
If you want a non-K-means alternative for the survey case, hierarchical clustering is often useful.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
7. Warnings
A segmentation that looks good in a notebook can still fail in practice if:

the features are built over inconsistent time windows,
the segments are not actionable,
the cluster solution is unstable over time,
one-hot encoded rare categories dominate the geometry,
outliers are left untreated,
stakeholders cannot name or use the resulting segments.

Good segmentation is not just a model-fitting exercise. It is a combination of:

sound feature engineering,
appropriate preprocessing,
sensible clustering,
careful profiling,
business interpretation.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
Pyodide and Quarto notes
This file is written as a normal Quarto .qmd using executable Python chunks. It can be rendered locally with Quarto and Jupyter. It is also designed to be Pyodide-friendly in the sense that it relies on packages that are commonly available in Pyodide-based browser execution environments.
A few practical notes:

Keep datasets modest in size for browser execution.
Prefer matplotlib over heavier plotting stacks.
Avoid file I/O dependencies.
Favor scikit-learn pipelines and transformers over niche clustering packages.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
Setup


Show code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from sklearn.base import clone
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans, AgglomerativeClustering
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, StandardScaler

RNG = np.random.default_rng(42)
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 100)




Show code
def evaluate_kmeans_range(X, k_values, random_state=42, n_init=20):
    rows = []
    for k in k_values:
        model = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=random_state, n_init=n_init)
        labels = model.fit_predict(X)
        rows.append({
            "k": k,
            "inertia": model.inertia_,
            "silhouette": silhouette_score(X, labels)
        })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)


def add_segment_order(summary_df, sort_col, ascending=True):
    ordered = summary_df.sort_values(sort_col, ascending=ascending).copy()
    ordered.insert(0, "segment_rank", range(1, len(ordered) + 1))
    return ordered


def plot_metric_curve(df_metrics, x_col="k", y_cols=("inertia", "silhouette"), title_prefix=""):
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

    axes[0].plot(df_metrics[x_col], df_metrics[y_cols[0]], marker="o")
    axes[0].set_title(f"{title_prefix}Inertia by k")
    axes[0].set_xlabel("k")
    axes[0].set_ylabel("Inertia")

    axes[1].plot(df_metrics[x_col], df_metrics[y_cols[1]], marker="o")
    axes[1].set_title(f"{title_prefix}Silhouette by k")
    axes[1].set_xlabel("k")
    axes[1].set_ylabel("Silhouette")

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


def plot_pca_segments(X, labels, title="PCA view of segments"):
    if hasattr(X, "toarray"):
        X = X.toarray()
    pca = PCA(n_components=2, random_state=42)
    coords = pca.fit_transform(X)

    plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
    scatter = plt.scatter(coords[:, 0], coords[:, 1], c=labels)
    plt.title(title)
    plt.xlabel("PC1")
    plt.ylabel("PC2")
    plt.show()


def top_categories_by_segment(df, segment_col, cat_cols):
    pieces = []
    for col in cat_cols:
        mode_vals = (
            df.groupby(segment_col)[col]
            .agg(lambda s: s.mode().iloc[0] if not s.mode().empty else np.nan)
            .rename(col)
        )
        pieces.append(mode_vals)
    return pd.concat(pieces, axis=1)

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
1. Survey segmentation

Framing
Survey segmentation is common when you have attitudinal or preference variables such as:

price sensitivity,
quality emphasis,
environmental concern,
novelty seeking,
channel preference.

In practice, survey segmentation often uses ordinal scales or one-hot encoded categories. To keep this tutorial Pyodide-friendly, we will build a synthetic survey with Likert-like numeric items and use K-means after standardization.


Generate synthetic survey data


Show code
n_per_segment = 120
segments = np.repeat(["Value Seekers", "Quality Loyalists", "Trend Explorers"], n_per_segment)

survey = pd.DataFrame({
    "true_segment": segments,
    "price_importance": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(4.6, 0.4, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(2.0, 0.5, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(3.0, 0.6, n_per_segment),
    ]),
    "quality_importance": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(2.3, 0.5, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(4.7, 0.4, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(3.8, 0.5, n_per_segment),
    ]),
    "innovation_interest": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(2.4, 0.5, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(3.0, 0.5, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(4.8, 0.3, n_per_segment),
    ]),
    "eco_concern": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(3.0, 0.7, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(4.0, 0.5, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(3.7, 0.6, n_per_segment),
    ]),
    "brand_loyalty": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(2.2, 0.6, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(4.6, 0.4, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(3.1, 0.6, n_per_segment),
    ]),
    "social_media_engagement": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(2.8, 0.7, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(2.2, 0.6, n_per_segment),
        RNG.normal(4.7, 0.4, n_per_segment),
    ])
})

likert_cols = [
    "price_importance", "quality_importance", "innovation_interest",
    "eco_concern", "brand_loyalty", "social_media_engagement"
]

survey[likert_cols] = survey[likert_cols].clip(1, 5).round(0)
survey.head()

A typical interpretation here would be:

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
2. Transaction segmentation

Framing
Transaction segmentation usually starts with customer-level feature engineering rather than raw order lines. Common features include:

recency,
purchase frequency,
monetary value,
average order value,
discount reliance,
category breadth.

This section generates synthetic RFM-style data plus two additional behavioral variables.


Generate synthetic transaction features


Show code
n_txn = 450
latent = np.repeat(["Dormant Discount", "Steady Midvalue", "High Value Loyal"], n_txn // 3)

transaction = pd.DataFrame({
    "true_segment": latent,
    "recency_days": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(220, 45, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(55, 18, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(14, 6, n_txn // 3),
    ]),
    "purchase_frequency": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(2.5, 1.0, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(10.0, 2.0, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(24.0, 4.0, n_txn // 3),
    ]),
    "monetary_value": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(120, 35, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(650, 120, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(2300, 350, n_txn // 3),
    ]),
    "discount_share": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(0.72, 0.10, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(0.28, 0.08, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(0.08, 0.04, n_txn // 3),
    ]),
    "category_breadth": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(1.8, 0.7, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(3.6, 0.9, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(5.8, 1.1, n_txn // 3),
    ]),
    "avg_order_value": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(48, 10, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(78, 14, n_txn // 3),
        RNG.normal(160, 25, n_txn // 3),
    ])
})

transaction["recency_days"] = transaction["recency_days"].clip(lower=1)
transaction["purchase_frequency"] = transaction["purchase_frequency"].clip(lower=1)
transaction["monetary_value"] = transaction["monetary_value"].clip(lower=10)
transaction["discount_share"] = transaction["discount_share"].clip(0, 1)
transaction["category_breadth"] = transaction["category_breadth"].clip(lower=1)
transaction["avg_order_value"] = transaction["avg_order_value"].clip(lower=5)

transaction.head()

transaction["avg_order_log"] = np.log1p(transaction["avg_order_value"])

transaction_features = [
    "recency_log", "purchase_frequency", "monetary_log",

Interpretation
A typical business reading might be:

high-recency, low-frequency, high-discount customers are at-risk bargain buyers,

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
3. CRM segmentation with mixed data

Framing
CRM and account-level segmentation often combines:

numeric variables such as tenure, spend, and support load,
categorical variables such as region, plan type, channel, and industry.

A mixed-type workflow can be handled cleanly with a ColumnTransformer that standardizes numeric columns and one-hot encodes categorical columns, followed by clustering.


Generate synthetic CRM data


Show code
n_crm = 480
crm_latent = np.repeat(["SMB Self-Serve", "Midmarket Partner", "Enterprise Direct"], n_crm // 3)

crm = pd.DataFrame({
    "true_segment": crm_latent,
    "annual_spend": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(900, 250, n_crm // 3),
        RNG.normal(6500, 1200, n_crm // 3),
        RNG.normal(28000, 5000, n_crm // 3),
    ]),
    "tenure_months": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(6, 3, n_crm // 3),
        RNG.normal(22, 8, n_crm // 3),
        RNG.normal(40, 10, n_crm // 3),
    ]),
    "support_tickets": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(1.5, 1.0, n_crm // 3),
        RNG.normal(4.2, 1.8, n_crm // 3),
        RNG.normal(9.0, 2.5, n_crm // 3),
    ]),
    "engagement_score": np.concatenate([
        RNG.normal(42, 10, n_crm // 3),
        RNG.normal(66, 9, n_crm // 3),
        RNG.normal(82, 7, n_crm // 3),
    ])
})

crm["annual_spend"] = crm["annual_spend"].clip(lower=100)
crm["tenure_months"] = crm["tenure_months"].clip(lower=1)
crm["support_tickets"] = crm["support_tickets"].clip(lower=0)
crm["engagement_score"] = crm["engagement_score"].clip(0, 100)

region_map = {
    "SMB Self-Serve": ["West", "South", "Midwest"],
    "Midmarket Partner": ["West", "Northeast", "Midwest"],
    "Enterprise Direct": ["Northeast", "West", "South"]
}
plan_map = {
    "SMB Self-Serve": ["Basic", "Standard"],
    "Midmarket Partner": ["Standard", "Premium"],
    "Enterprise Direct": ["Premium", "Enterprise"]
}
channel_map = {
    "SMB Self-Serve": ["Web", "Product-Led"],
    "Midmarket Partner": ["Partner", "Sales"],
    "Enterprise Direct": ["Sales", "Field"]
}
industry_map = {
    "SMB Self-Serve": ["Retail", "Services", "Education"],
    "Midmarket Partner": ["Manufacturing", "Healthcare", "Services"],
    "Enterprise Direct": ["Finance", "Technology", "Healthcare"]
}

crm["region"] = [RNG.choice(region_map[s]) for s in crm["true_segment"]]
crm["plan_type"] = [RNG.choice(plan_map[s]) for s in crm["true_segment"]]
crm["acquisition_channel"] = [RNG.choice(channel_map[s]) for s in crm["true_segment"]]
crm["industry"] = [RNG.choice(industry_map[s]) for s in crm["true_segment"]]

crm.head()

Build the preprocessing pipeline


Show code
crm_num = ["annual_spend", "tenure_months", "support_tickets", "engagement_score"]
crm_cat = ["region", "plan_type", "acquisition_channel", "industry"]

crm_preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    transformers=[
        (
            "num",
            Pipeline([
                ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
                ("scaler", StandardScaler())
            ]),
            crm_num,
        ),
        (
            "cat",
            Pipeline([
                ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
                ("onehot", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"))
            ]),
            crm_cat,
        )
    ]
)

X_crm = crm_preprocessor.fit_transform(crm[crm_num + crm_cat])
X_crm.shape


(480, 24)

comparison = pd.DataFrame({

    "silhouette_at_k": [
        float(survey_metrics.loc[survey_metrics["k"] == k_survey, "silhouette"].iloc[0]),
        float(transaction_metrics.loc[transaction_metrics["k"] == k_txn, "silhouette"].iloc[0]),
        float(crm_metrics.loc[crm_metrics["k"] == k_crm, "silhouette"].iloc[0]),

}).round(3)

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
5. Recommended adaptation patterns

For real survey data
Replace the synthetic survey with your real table and:

map Likert items to numeric scores,
consider one-hot encoding for purely nominal variables,
compare KMeans with AgglomerativeClustering.



For real transaction data
Build a customer-feature table first:

recency,
purchase frequency,
spend,
average order value,
category breadth,
discount share,
return rate,
tenure.

Then:

log-transform highly skewed variables,
standardize,
test multiple values of k,
profile the segments in business language.



For real CRM data
Use a mixed-data preprocessing pipeline:

impute missing values,
scale numerics,
one-hot encode categoricals,
cluster transformed features,
summarize both means and top categories by segment.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
section: 6. Optional: hierarchical clustering for survey data
text:
6. Optional: hierarchical clustering for survey data
If you want a non-K-means alternative for the survey case, hierarchical clustering is often useful.


Show code
survey_hier = AgglomerativeClustering(n_clusters=3)
survey["segment_hier"] = survey_hier.fit_predict(X_survey)

pd.crosstab(survey["segment_hier"], survey["true_segment"])








true_segment

Trend Explorers

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
section: 7. What to watch for in production work
text:
7. What to watch for in production work
A segmentation that looks good in a notebook can still fail in practice if:

the features are built over inconsistent time windows,
the segments are not actionable,
the cluster solution is unstable over time,
one-hot encoded rare categories dominate the geometry,
outliers are left untreated,
stakeholders cannot name or use the resulting segments.

Good segmentation is not just a model-fitting exercise. It is a combination of:

sound feature engineering,
appropriate preprocessing,
sensible clustering,
careful profiling,
business interpretation.

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
8. Session info

import sys
import sklearn

print("Python:", sys.version.split()[0])

print("scikit-learn:", sklearn.__version__)

title: Market Segmentation in Python: A Quarto Tutorial with Synthetic Data
text:
9. Rendering notes
Render locally with Quarto using your usual workflow, for example:
quarto render market_segmentation_pyodide_tutorial.qmd
For browser-side Python deployment, keep the code modest and use a Pyodide-compatible publishing workflow.

title: Deployment Guide
text:
Quarto supports publishing websites to GitHub Pages by rendering the site locally into a docs/ directory and then configuring GitHub Pages to publish from that directory. citeturn580333search2
That is the pattern used in this project:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
This is a good default because it keeps the source files readable and the published output explicit.

title: Deployment Guide
text:
Quarto supports publishing websites to GitHub Pages by rendering the site locally into a docs/ directory and then configuring GitHub Pages to publish from that directory. citeturn580333search2
That is the pattern used in this project:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
This is a good default because it keeps the source files readable and the published output explicit.

title: Deployment Guide
section: 2. Why keep the main tutorial static?
text:
2. Why keep the main tutorial static?
The main segmentation tutorial uses executable Python chunks but is primarily a teaching document, not a full application. Keeping it static makes GitHub Pages deployment simple and robust.
Quarto websites share navigation and rendering options across pages through _quarto.yml, which makes this mini-project easy to grow into a larger teaching site. citeturn580333search8turn580333search17

title: Deployment Guide
text:
3. Browser-side interactive pattern
When you want sliders or lightweight interaction without hosting a Python server, the cleanest approach is a browser-side pattern using Shinylive. The Shinylive Quarto extension is designed to embed Shiny for Python applications inside Quarto documents and run Python in the browser. citeturn231216view0
This is useful for small interactive controls such as:

changing the number of clusters,
changing synthetic sample size,
switching feature pairs,
showing the effect of scaling.

title: Deployment Guide
text:
4. Server-backed versus browser-backed interactivity
Quarto’s Shiny for Python pages can also run in the more traditional server: shiny mode, but that requires a Python-serving environment rather than plain static hosting. Quarto’s docs note that standard Shiny interactive documents are generated as an HTML page plus an app.py and are typically deployed to a server-capable environment. citeturn407962search2
GitHub Pages is static hosting, so the browser-side pattern is the better fit there.

title: Deployment Guide
text:
5. Install the optional Shinylive extension
From the project root:
quarto add quarto-ext/shinylive
Quarto stores extensions locally in an _extensions directory within the project, and the Quarto docs recommend checking that directory into version control for reproducibility. citeturn720128search6

title: Deployment Guide
text:
6. Preview locally
For the static site:
quarto preview
For Shiny for Python documents more generally, Quarto supports preview from the editor or command line with quarto preview. citeturn407962search2

title: Deployment Guide
text:
7. Publish with GitHub Pages
You have two practical options:

Option A: Commit docs/

Run quarto render
Commit the generated docs/
In GitHub repository settings, publish from docs/ on the default branch



Option B: Use GitHub Actions
This project includes a starter workflow in .github/workflows/publish.yml.
Quarto documents GitHub Actions as a supported CI path for publishing. citeturn580333search20

title: Deployment Guide
text:
8. Replace placeholders before publishing
Edit these placeholders in _quarto.yml:

YOUR-USERNAME
YOUR-REPO

The site-url matters for some website features, including feeds and canonical site behavior. Quarto notes that site-url should be set for generated feeds and website features that depend on the final URL. citeturn580333search21
